choices.prompt: A Synapse dedicated SQL pool is described as a massively parallel processing (MPP) engine where a table is split across distributions and a query runs as many parallel tasks. Given this design, which kind of operation does the module say the engine handles well, and why?
choices: Set-based operations like INSERT ... SELECT, UPDATE ... FROM, and MERGE, because each distribution works its slice in parallel
Row-by-row cursor loops, because each row is dispatched to its own distribution for isolation
Single-threaded transactions, because serializing work guarantees consistency across distributions
Iterative procedural logic, because the engine replays each iteration on every distribution simultaneously
correct_answers: Set-based operations like INSERT ... SELECT, UPDATE ... FROM, and MERGE, because each distribution works its slice in parallel

choices.prompt: A nightly job loads 50 million rows into a dedicated SQL pool using a loop of INSERT statements and runs for hours. According to the module, what load pattern should replace it and why is it faster?
choices: External tables plus CTAS (or COPY INTO), because they read files in parallel directly from storage and load minimally-logged
A single MERGE statement against the source files, because MERGE skips logging entirely on an MPP engine
A cursor that batches the INSERTs in groups of 10,000, because batching converts row-by-row work into set-based work
Streaming the rows through the client with a faster driver, because client-side parallelism bypasses the distributions
correct_answers: External tables plus CTAS (or COPY INTO), because they read files in parallel directly from storage and load minimally-logged

choices.prompt: The morning join of two large fact tables shows a large SHUFFLE_MOVE in the query plan. Per the module, what physical design change removes the shuffle?
choices: Hash-distribute both tables on the column they join on, so matching rows already live on the same distribution
Replicate both large fact tables so every distribution has a full copy and no movement is needed
Add a clustered columnstore index to both tables so the optimizer caches the join result
Rewrite the join as a cursor so rows are compared one pair at a time without movement
correct_answers: Hash-distribute both tables on the column they join on, so matching rows already live on the same distribution

choices.prompt: In the walkthrough, the outer OPENJSON extracts a header property defined as `items NVARCHAR(MAX) '$.items' AS JSON`. What is the specific purpose of the `AS JSON` modifier, and what happens if it is omitted?
choices: It keeps the nested array as a JSON blob so a second OPENJSON can shred it; without it, the inner array cannot be handed to a second OPENJSON and the nested shred fails or returns nulls
It validates that the document is well-formed JSON before parsing; without it, OPENJSON raises a parse error on the whole document
It forces the column to be stored compressed; without it, the staging table grows too large to CTAS
It converts the array into a comma-separated string; without it, CROSS APPLY produces one row per character instead of per element
correct_answers: It keeps the nested array as a JSON blob so a second OPENJSON can shred it; without it, the inner array cannot be handed to a second OPENJSON and the nested shred fails or returns nulls

choices.prompt: According to the module, which statements about CTAS (CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT) as a load pattern in a dedicated SQL pool are true? Select all that apply.
choices: CTAS creates a new physical table and populates it in one parallel, minimally-logged operation
CTAS lets you set the table's distribution and index at creation time
CTAS streams rows one statement at a time through the client, which is why it is preferred over INSERT
The idiom is external table → SELECT with your transforms → CTAS into the target with the right distribution
correct_answers: CTAS creates a new physical table and populates it in one parallel, minimally-logged operation
CTAS lets you set the table's distribution and index at creation time
The idiom is external table → SELECT with your transforms → CTAS into the target with the right distribution

choices.prompt: The module says you hash-distribute large fact tables on the common join/group key. What does it recommend you do with small dimension tables, and why?
choices: Replicate them so a full copy exists on every distribution, so joins to them need no cross-distribution movement
Hash-distribute them on the same key as the fact table so both shuffle together
Load them with a cursor since they are small enough that row-by-row work is acceptable
Store them only as external tables so they are read fresh from the lake on every join
correct_answers: Replicate them so a full copy exists on every distribution, so joins to them need no cross-distribution movement

choices.prompt: According to the module, what is the underlying cause of a SHUFFLE_MOVE appearing in a dedicated SQL pool query plan?
choices: A join or aggregation needs rows that live on different distributions, so the engine moves them across the network
The table has a clustered columnstore index that must be rebuilt before each scan
OPENJSON has to re-parse the JSON documents once per distribution
The loader decoded the source files with the wrong code page, forcing a re-read
correct_answers: A join or aggregation needs rows that live on different distributions, so the engine moves them across the network

choices.prompt: The module explains that 'café' showing up as 'cafÃ©' is mojibake. What does it identify as the root cause of this corruption?
choices: A file is bytes, and a UTF-8 file was read with the wrong code page (such as Latin-1), so the bytes decode into the wrong characters
The accented characters were stored as NULL because the column type was too narrow
CROSS APPLY exploded the string into one row per character and reassembled it incorrectly
The CTAS operation compressed the column and lost the high bytes during minimal logging
correct_answers: A file is bytes, and a UTF-8 file was read with the wrong code page (such as Latin-1), so the bytes decode into the wrong characters

choices.prompt: In the walkthrough that shreds the supplier delivery documents, which statements accurately describe how the JSON is turned into rows? Select all that apply.
choices: The outer OPENJSON uses a WITH clause to extract typed header columns and pull the items array out as a JSON blob
CROSS APPLY runs the shred once per parent row, exploding the nested array into one row per element
A second OPENJSON over CROSS APPLY explodes the items array into one row per line item with its own WITH schema
OPENJSON returns a single scalar value, so each document must be looped over with a cursor to produce rows
correct_answers: The outer OPENJSON uses a WITH clause to extract typed header columns and pull the items array out as a JSON blob
CROSS APPLY runs the shred once per parent row, exploding the nested array into one row per element
A second OPENJSON over CROSS APPLY explodes the items array into one row per line item with its own WITH schema

choices.prompt: The module lists several common pitfalls. Which of the following does it explicitly call out as anti-patterns to avoid? Select all that apply.
choices: Using row-by-row cursors in an MPP pool instead of a single set-based statement
Patching mojibake with REPLACE chains rather than fixing the decode at load time
Joining two large tables distributed on different keys, triggering a costly shuffle every run
Adding a clustered columnstore index to a gold table that the dashboards scan analytically
correct_answers: Using row-by-row cursors in an MPP pool instead of a single set-based statement
Patching mojibake with REPLACE chains rather than fixing the decode at load time
Joining two large tables distributed on different keys, triggering a costly shuffle every run

llm.prompt: Explain why row-by-row cursors are an anti-pattern in a Synapse dedicated SQL pool, and describe how the MPP architecture changes the way you should express transformations.
llm.reference_answer: A dedicated SQL pool is a massively parallel processing (MPP) engine: the table is split across distributions and a query runs as many parallel tasks, one per distribution, so each distribution works its slice simultaneously. Set-based operations like INSERT ... SELECT, UPDATE ... FROM, and MERGE exploit this parallelism, while cursors and row-by-row loops serialize work the engine wants to parallelize and can run orders of magnitude slower. The correct approach is to express the transformation as a single set-based statement rather than iterating. So instead of a cursor you rewrite the logic as MERGE, UPDATE ... FROM, or CTAS to let every distribution process its rows in parallel.

llm.prompt: Walk through how you would load JSON supplier delivery documents (a header plus a nested items array) from the lake into a curated, hash-distributed fact table, naming each T-SQL mechanism you would use and what it does.
llm.reference_answer: First define an external table that points at the JSON files in the lake (via the PolyBase-style external table mechanism or COPY INTO) so the engine reads them in parallel directly from storage. Then shred the JSON with OPENJSON: an outer OPENJSON with a WITH clause extracts the typed header columns and pulls the items array out as a JSON blob using AS JSON, and a CROSS APPLY OPENJSON over that array explodes it into one row per line item with its own WITH schema casting each value. Finally use CTAS (CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT) to materialize the result into the gold fact table in one parallel, minimally-logged operation, setting DISTRIBUTION = HASH(product_id) so it hashes on the join key and adding a clustered columnstore index so the dashboards' analytical scans are fast. Hash-distributing on product_id (the column joined to the product dimension) means later joins avoid a shuffle.

llm.prompt: Some product names arrive as mojibake (for example 'café' shows up as 'cafÃ©'). Explain why this happens and compare the durable fix the module recommends against patching the strings with REPLACE chains.
llm.reference_answer: A file is just bytes, and turning bytes into characters requires the correct code page; when a UTF-8 file is read as Latin-1 (or vice versa), accented characters decode wrong and become mojibake. The fix lives at load time: tell the loader the source encoding so it decodes correctly, since COPY INTO and external file formats accept an encoding setting, and when the source is genuinely mixed the durable answer is to standardize on UTF-8 upstream. Patching the symptoms with REPLACE chains is brittle whack-a-mole that never covers every character. The module's guidance is to fix the decode, not each symptom.
